title: 特斯拉利润突破 10 亿美元； “公众对电动汽车的情绪和支持似乎处于前所未有的拐点，”
title_en: Tesla tops $1 billion in profit; “Public sentiment and support for electric vehicles seems to be at a never-before-seen inflection point,”

note:
我一直想赚很多钱，让人们关注我，做很多令人兴奋的事情。我只是不知道怎么做。我的许多创办自己公司的朋友告诉我，他们小时候是如何表现出企业家精神的——他们从背包里卖糖果，在夏天做园艺生意等等。他们创造了价值，学习了努力的美德。早点工作。但那不是我。我在曼哈顿海滩中学的电脑教室用 Applesoft BASIC 创建了一个赛马模拟游戏，并经营了一个小型赌博业务。我的意思是，谁能怪我？老师们在课间休息和午餐时完全无人看管，好像他们无法理解任何孩子怎么会在玩那些大型计算型打字机时遇到麻烦。然而，花几美元购买源源不断的 Atari 游戏并学习如何从更大的 8 年级学生那里收集并没有教会我任何关于建立一家科技公司的知识。当我决定搬到旧金山时，我已经完全无能为力了。同样糟糕的是我的时间感。当我进入就业市场时，互联网泡沫已经破灭，数十名聪明人失业，一波风险投资公司破产。很多人离开湾区回到他们来自的地方，但我太愚蠢了，不知道人们做出的合乎逻辑的决定。我所在的折扣经纪公司为我们提供了为期两周的培训，内容是如何在他们位于圣地亚哥的呼叫中心进行在线股票交易。那时我有了我的绝妙主意（除非我更改了几次才证明它很出色）。负责监督我们培训的呼叫中心经理已经 20 多岁了，他的脸上每天都挂着得意的笑容。 “我教你的这个呼叫中心系统每人要花费 30,000 美元，”他多次提到。他笑得很开心，我很鄙视他。
我突然想到，如果我能开发出更便宜的呼叫中心软件，我就可以创建自己的软件公司——并报复 The Smirker。星星一定是在为我对齐，因为此后不久，我的大学室友，我昵称他为“The Fro”（我给所有我深爱的人都起了昵称），打电话告诉我（吹嘘）呼叫中心软件他工作的初创公司被思科收购。他承认，作为一名后期员工，他并没有赚到多少，但他对可能的东西有所了解，并鼓励我飞往波士顿讨论创建我们自己的创业公司。 “毕竟，”他说，“你很擅长卖狗屎。”我不确定这是恭维，但我买了机票。经过一个周末讨论梦想和男人的感觉后，我们彼此都很兴奋，决定在下周一辞去工作。我给了我两周的通知。然后奇怪的事情发生了。那个星期一一整天都不接我电话。星期三，他终于给我打电话，害羞地说：“Boooo。你知道什么兄弟吗？我决定现在辞职对我来说不是一个好主意。严重地。我很抱歉。”我的银行账户里有 5,000 美元，我认真地想过花 1,000 美元只是为了飞回波士顿打他的脸。相反，我挂了电话，敲了敲墙。也许几次。
然后我给他回电，尽可能冷静地问他，他是否知道他新收购的初创公司中还有其他人可能想和我一起创建一家软件公司。我提醒他我很擅长卖狗屎。他向我介绍了“Tooter”，结果证明他正带我去 Tahoe 去滑雪。当我们在天堂滑雪胜地见面时，我发现了一个明显聪明的家伙，他不喜欢公司生活，并且对在加利福尼亚州可能不完全合法的活动有着肆无忌惮的感情。但是，嘿，我们都有问题。他向我解释说，他和 The Fro 曾在一家为呼叫中心开发软件的初创公司工作，他们通过在公司名称中添加 .com 彻底改变了这个古老的行业。因为我现在是一名企业家（失业），我告诉他我们应该重新创建那种软件，他同意了。值得称赞的是，让我有些惊讶的是，他给他的老板发了一封电子邮件，并在那天晚上辞职了。几周后，他卖掉了他在波士顿的房子，并在湾区买了一套新的。但让他成为如此冒险者和出色程序员的原因有时也让他成为了一个超级混蛋。对软件公司一无所知，我的许多问题都遇到了他自己的问题，他最喜欢的是，“你他妈的白痴吗？”独自度过了那么多令人沮丧的夜晚，我经常会无意识地一遍又一遍地看我最喜欢的电影《肖申克的救赎》。也许看到 Andy Dufresne 忍受如此多的痛苦让我对自己的处境感觉更好。显然，我需要帮助（治疗），所以我问我在折扣经纪公司的前老板是否知道任何天使投资人。
谢天谢地，他向我介绍了他最喜欢的日间交易客户，并向他解释说我是一个很有前途的企业家，图特是一位了不起的工程师，他绝对是。我尽可能热情地向投资者解释说，我要么建立一家成功的软件公司，要么尝试死亡，而且我要自我保护，所以…… 日内交易员指着他办公室墙上的一架小型喷气式飞机的照片说：“我要你帮我买这个。”然后他当场给我们开了一张 15 万美元的支票，我们得到了正式的资助。然后我们聘请了“机器”（工程师 #2），我和他一起搬进了旧金山双峰附近的一间公寓，主要是为了我可以日夜骚扰他编程。当我的两个工程师构建了我们的呼叫中心软件的测试版时，我开始拨通电话，向呼叫中心所有者推销更便宜的东西。在打了 2000 多个电话之后，我终于在另一条线路上听到了一个接听的声音。乔在犹他州普罗沃拥有一家规模虽小但很成功的呼叫中心。他说我虚张声势，说他知道我们还没有任何坚固的东西，但他相信我们可以建造它——但它必须是我给他报价的一半。这就是我学到的关于创业的早期教训，或者它可能是美国本身的教训。在被拒绝了这么多次之后，我想拒绝自己，但后来找到了一个即使在经济衰退期间也会给我寄一张 40,000 美元支票的人，因为他们本身就是冒险的企业主。这让我意识到美国对企业家来说是一个神奇的地方。甚至是无知的人。有了那张 40,000 美元的支票，我们从折扣经纪公司聘请了我的前任老板，从被思科收购的呼叫中心初创公司聘请了一名销售代表，我们五个人去核桃溪的一个小办公室工作。
我们开始运送装有 Dialogic 电话板的戴尔机器，里面装有我们的呼叫中心软件。它并不性感，但足以支撑我们雇用更多人并开始发展。与其构建软件加载到廉价服务器上并像其他人一样销售，为什么不构建一个我们自己托管的“大型服务器”，让人们通过网络租用功能？我的前老板告诉我，“不，那太愚蠢了，这是在杀死金鹅。”工程师告诉我，“不，这会很麻烦。”他们向我发送了来自知名分析师的各种研究，表明“ASP”（被“SaaS”取代之前的流行语）毕竟不是一种有利可图的商业模式。我说：“伙计们，还有什么选择？你想在你的余生中成为这些垃圾箱的低成本领导者吗？”我指着我们小办公室里越来越多的戴尔盒子。与他们激烈争论了一个月，我意识到按需软件服务没有利润，因为每个人都只是试图采用传统的客户端 - 服务器软件并自己托管它。他们没有想过要构建真正的多租户的东西，这意味着构建专门用于将多个客户和帐户作为服务处理的软件。如果我们能够实现它，那对我们来说将是一个关键优势，因为它需要购买的硬件显着减少，并且更容易管理客户帐户。
但还有另一个问题。与我们的 150,000 美元相比，该领域的少数其他初创公司总共筹集了超过 1 亿美元，而且他们正在领先。就像，前进的道路。然后有一天 The Machine 给了我一个 IP 语音电话盒让我玩。我们都喜欢小工具，我们只是觉得能够通过互联网免费拨打长途电话真是太酷了。就在那时，我遇到了 Eureka 2.0 时刻——我们的竞争对手拥有需要大量硬件和运营支持来大规模服务客户的单租户解决方案，但他们也不得不将客户的长途话费翻倍。由于 SaaS 呼叫中心产品必须为每个呼叫中​​心座席提供三个持续的电话连接（而在内部部署模型中则是传统的两个），因此它消耗了客户迁移到 SaaS 时所节省的大部分成本。基于 IP 语音的服务将为我们带来另一个巨大的市场优势，因为客户实际上可以降低他们的电话费。很少有人考虑多租户和 IP 语音，但我终于开始寻找线索。我有幸获得了图特、机器和我的前任老板的好运，他们最终以他们可观的超能力团结在我的愿景周围。直到今天，我都非常感谢他们每个人。对我来说，那个“决定性的一天”是在 Safeway 杂货店（我经常担心借记卡被退回），当时 The Machine 用我的黑莓手机给我打电话。 “伙计，伙计，伙计。猜猜我从哪里打来的电话？”机器问道。
“呃，我们的公寓？”我开始在收银台阅读《人物》杂志的封面。布列塔尼斯皮尔斯正在和一个新人约会。 “是的，”机器兴奋地说，“但这是我从我们的 IP 语音堆栈中进行的第一个调用。听起来不错，对吧？”我双膝跪地，简直是被打倒了。我们仍然面临着如此多的障碍，我知道这一点，但在那一刻，也是有史以来第一次，我对我们将粉碎它充满信心。竞争者，怀疑者，每个人。我们打算摧毁这个行业，然后以我们自己的形象重塑它。这就是我们曲棍球棒曲线的真正开始。我们终于找到了一种方法来提供一种更好 10 倍且便宜 5 倍的产品。我接到第一个 IP 语音电话的 Safeway Express Checkout 线路让我想起了《肖申克的救赎》中的那个场景，主角爬过数英里的污水管，然后终于能够挣脱了。不久之后，我们正式上线（新建网站）“虚拟呼叫中心”，业务持续高速增长。我们的竞争对手大多没有回复我之前的电子邮件，询问我是否可以在城里给他们买午餐，他们突然对我们的价格如此之低感到震惊。具有讽刺意味的是，我们最终成为了低成本的领导者，这是我们可以做到的，因为我们的内在成本要低得多。我们从信任我们的大公司那里获得了大量风险投资，最后，一个伟大的办公空间给所有人留下了深刻的印象，除了我父亲——他一直问我为什么还不是医生。
第一年我们的收入为 0 美元，第二年为 90 万美元，第二年为 300 万美元。在我离开之前，我带领公司每年的经常性收入达到了 1000 万美元。事实是，公司已经成为自己的实体，不再需要我了。今天，我命名为 Five9 的公司在纳斯达克股票市场交易，雇佣了数百名加州湾区人，首次公开募股使该公司在开业当天的估值达到 3.5 亿美元。每当我致电使用我的软件的公司呼叫中心时，我都会忍不住微笑。直到我被搁置超过两分钟。我不确定我是否对其他企业家有任何明智的建议，但从这次经历中我确实学到了一个重要的真理。也就是说，您不必是常春藤盟校毕业生（我不是）或有很多钱（我们没有）。你只需要相信自己反对所有合理的逻辑，尽管听起来很陈腐。相信即使你做了一些愚蠢的事情，比如毫无头绪地辞职，但无论如何你都会弄清楚的。相信在经历了多年监狱般的辛劳创业生活之后，你会从数英里的污水管道中冒出来，感受雨水冲刷掉你脸上的垃圾。你会带着一袋钱消失在墨西哥的海滩上，与你的同谋见面并喝玛格丽塔酒。
note_en:
I’ve always wanted to make a lot of money, have people pay a lot of attention to me and do a lot of exciting things. I just never knew how.
 Many of my friends who are founders of their own companies tell me how they exhibited the entrepreneurial spirit as a kid — they sold candy out of their backpacks, had a landscaping business during the summer, etc. They  created value and learned the virtues of hard work early on.
 But that wasn’t me. I  created a horse-racing simulation game in Applesoft BASIC in Manhattan Beach Middle School’s computer classroom and ran a small gambling operation. I mean, who could blame me? The teachers left that class completely unattended during recess and lunch, as if they couldn’t fathom how any kid could get into trouble playing with those large calculating typewriters.
 However, making a few dollars to buy an endless stream of Atari games and learning how to collect from the bigger 8th graders didn’t teach me anything about building a tech  company. By the time I decided to move to San Francisco, I was completely and totally clueless.
 Just as bad was my sense of timing. By the time I entered the job market the dot-com bubble had burst, leaving scores of smart people unemployed and a wave of VC firms bust. A lot of people were leaving the Bay Area to go back to wherever they had come from, but I was too stupid to know anything about the logical decisions people made.
 The discount brokerage firm where I clerked gave us all two weeks of training in how to execute online stock trades at one of their call centers in San Diego. That’s when I had my brilliant idea (except it didn’t turn out to be brilliant until I changed it a few times).
 The call center manager who monitored our training was in his late twenties and had a smug grin on his face all day, every day. “This call center system I’m teaching you costs $30,000 a person,” he mentioned, several times. He smirked a lot and I despised him.
 It struck me that if I could build cheaper call center  software, I could make my own  software  company — and have revenge on The Smirker.
 The stars must have been aligning for me because shortly thereafter, my college roommate, who I nicknamed “The Fro” (I give nicknames to everyone for whom I have a deep affection), called to tell me (brag) that the call center  software startup he worked for had been acquired by Cisco.
 He hadn’t made much as a late-stage employee, he admitted, but he had a taste for what could be, and encouraged me to fly to Boston to discuss creating our own startup. “After all,” he said, “you’re good at selling shit.”
 I wasn’t sure that was a compliment, but I bought the plane ticket.
 After a weekend of discussing dreams and man-feelings, we got each other excited enough to decide to quit our jobs the following Monday. I gave my two weeks notice.
  Then something odd happened. The Fro wouldn’t answer my calls all day that Monday.   On Wednesday he finally called me and said sheepishly, “Brooo. You know what Bro? I decided that it’s not a good idea for me to quit my job right now. Seriously. I’m so sorry.”
 I had $5,000 in my bank account, and I seriously thought of spending $1,000 of it just to fly back to Boston to punch him in the face. Instead, I hung up the phone and punched the wall. Maybe a few times.
 Then I called him back and asked him, as calmly as I could, if he knew of anyone else at his newly acquired startup that might want to create a  software  company with me. I reminded him that I was good at selling shit. He introduced me to “Tooter,” who, it turned out, was heading my way to go snowboarding in Tahoe.
 When we met at Heavenly Ski Resort I discovered an obviously brilliant guy who disliked corporate life and had an unbridled affection for activities that may not have been fully legal in the state of California. But hey, we all had issues.
 He explained to me that he and The Fro had worked at a startup that  created  software for call centers, an ages-old industry they had revolutionized by adding a .com to the name of the  company.
 Because I was an entrepreneur (unemployed) now, I told him that we should just recreate that class of  software, and he agreed. To his credit, and somewhat to my amazement, he sent an email to his boss and quit that night. A few weeks later he sold his house in Boston and bought a new one in the Bay Area.
 But what made him such a risk taker and brilliant programmer also made him a supreme asshole at times.  Knowing  nothing about  software companies, many of my questions were met with a question of his own, his favorite being, “Are you a fucking idiot?”
 There were so many depressing nights alone that often I would watch my favorite movie, The Shawshank Redemption, over and over, mindlessly. Maybe seeing Andy Dufresne enduring so much shit made me feel better about my own situation.
 Clearly, I needed help (therapy), so I asked my former boss at the discount brokerage firm if he knew of any angel investors.
 Thankfully, he introduced me to his favorite day-trader client and explained to him that I was a promising entrepreneur and that Tooter was an amazing engineer, which he absolutely was. I explained to the investor as passionately as I could that I was going to build a successful  software  company or die trying, and that I was into self-preservation, so…
 The day trader pointed to a picture of a small jet on his office wall and said, “I want you to help me buy this.” He then wrote us a check for $150,000 on the spot, and we were officially funded.
 We then hired “The Machine” (Engineer #2) and I moved in with him into an apartment in the Twin Peaks neighborhood of San Francisco, mostly so I could harass him into programming day and night. And as my two engineers built a beta version of our call center  software, I started dialing for dollars and calling call center owners with the pitch and promise of something cheaper.
  After what must have been more than 2,000 cold calls, I finally reached a receptive voice on the other line. Joe owned a small but successful call center in Provo, Utah. He called my bluff and said that he knew we didn’t have anything solid yet, but he trusted we could get it built — but it had to be half the price I quoted him.
 And that’s the early lesson I learned about entrepreneurship, or maybe it was a lesson in America itself. That after so many rejections, I wanted to reject myself, but then found someone who would mail me a check for $40,000, even during a recession, because they were risk-taking business owners themselves.
 It made me realize that America was a magical place for entrepreneurs. Even the clueless ones.
 With that $40,000 check we hired my former boss from the discount brokerage firm, hired a sales rep from the call center startup that had been acquired by Cisco and the five of us went to work in a small office in Walnut Creek.
 We started shipping Dell machines with a Dialogic telephony board inside loaded with our call center  software. It wasn’t sexy, but it sustained us enough to hire a few more people and start growing.
  Instead of building  software to load on cheap servers to sell like everyone else, why not build one “mega-server” that we host ourselves and let people rent the functionality over the web?
 My former boss told me, “No, that’s stupid, that’s killing the Golden Goose.”
 The engineers told me, “No, that’s going to be a pain in the ass to build.” They sent me all sorts of studies from reputable analysts showing that “ASPs” (the buzzword before it was replaced by “SaaS”) was not a profitable business model after all.
 I said, “Guys, what’s the alternative? You want to be the low-cost leader for the rest of your lives hustling these shit-boxes?” I pointed to the growing pile of Dell boxes accumulating in our small office.
 Arguing vehemently with them for a month, I realized that on-demand  software services weren’t profitable because everyone had just tried to take traditional client-server  software and host it themselves.
  They hadn’t thought to build something that was truly multi-tenant, meaning building  software that was solely designed to handle multiple clients and accounts as a service. That would be a critical advantage for us, if we could pull it off, as it was significantly less hardware to purchase and much easier to manage customer accounts.
 But there was another problem. A handful of other startups in this space had collectively raised more than $100  million dollars, compared to our $150,000, and they were pulling ahead. Like, way ahead.
 Then one day The Machine gave me a Voice Over IP telephony box to play with. We both loved gadgets and we just thought it was so cool to be able to make long distance calls for free over the Internet.
 And that’s when my Eureka 2.0 moment hit me — our competitors had single-tenant solutions requiring massive amounts of hardware and operational support to service customers at scale, but they also had to double their customers’ long distance bills.
 Because a SaaS call center product has to have three phone connections ongoing per each call center agent (versus the traditional two in an on-premise model), it ate into much of the cost savings a customer would experience moving to SaaS. A service based on Voice Over IP would give us another tremendous advantage in the marketplace, as customers could actually lower their phone bills.
 Few others were thinking about multi-tenancy and Voice Over IP, but I was finally starting to buy clues. And I had the good fortune of Tooter, The Machine and my former boss eventually rallying around my vision with their considerable super powers. To this day, I’m so thankful for each of them.
 That “fateful day” for me was at a Safeway grocery store (where I would often worry about my debit card bouncing) when The Machine called me on my BlackBerry.
 “Dude, dude, dude. Guess where I’m calling from?” The Machine asked.
 “Uh, our apartment?” I started reading the cover of People magazine in the checkout line. Brittany Spears was dating someone new.
 “Yeah,” said The Machine excitedly, “but this is the first call I’m making from our Voice Over IP stack. Sounds fucking good, right?”
 I dropped to my knees, literally floored. We faced so many obstacles still, I knew that, but at that moment, and for the first time ever, I felt supremely confident that we were going to crush it. The competitors, the doubters, everyone. We were going to destroy the industry, then reinvent it in our own image.
 And that’s really how the curve in our hockey stick began. We finally figured out a way to deliver a product that was 10x better and 5x cheaper.
 That Safeway Express Checkout line where I got our first Voice Over IP call reminded me of that scene in The Shawshank Redemption where the main character crawls through miles of sewage pipe before finally being able to break free.
 Shortly afterwards, we officially launched (built a new website) “The Virtual Call Center” and our business kept growing at an increasingly rapid pace. Our competitors, most of whom had not replied to my previous emails asking if I could buy them lunch in the city, were suddenly stunned at how low our prices were.
  Ironically, we did end up being the low-cost leader, something we could do because our intrinsic costs were so much lower. We got substantial VC funding from great firms who believed in us and, finally, a great office space that impressed everyone except my father  — who kept asking me why I wasn’t a doctor yet.
 The first year we had done $0 in revenues, the next year $900,000 and the next year $3 million. I led the  company to $10 million in annually recurring revenues before I departed.  The truth was, the  company had become its own entity and didn’t need me anymore.
 Today, the  company I named  Five9 is traded on the NASDAQ stock market and employs hundreds of Bay Area Californians, with an IPO that gave the  company an opening day valuation of  $350  million dollars. Whenever I call into a corporation’s call center that’s using my  software, I can’t help but smile. Until I’m on hold for longer than two minutes.
 I’m not sure if I have any sage advice for other entrepreneurs, but out of this experience I did learn one important truth. Namely, you don’t have to be an Ivy League graduate (I’m not) or have a lot of money (we didn’t). You just have to believe in yourself against all reasonable logic, as trite as that sounds.
 Believe that even if you do something stupid like quit your job without a clue, somehow you’re going to figure it out. Believe that after enduring years of the prison-like toil entrepreneurial life can be, you will emerge from miles of sewage pipes to feel the rain wash the crap off your face.
 And that you will disappear to a beach in Mexico with a bag full of money, meet up with your co-conspirators and have margaritas.
title: 我创建了一家价值 3.5 亿美元的软件公司，对软件一无所知
title_en: I Created a $350M Software Company Knowing Nothing About Software

埃德加·爱伦·坡 (1809-1849) 美国作家。 （环球历史档案/盖蒂图片社）“科学！...”开篇一首诗始于 1820 年代后期，由年轻的埃德加·爱伦·坡 (Edgar Allan Poe) 创作，并在他的一生中不断修改。 “你为什么要攻击诗人的心，秃鹫！它的翅膀是沉闷的现实！”坡，即将成为西点军校学生，学习数学、几何和工程，悲伤地继续说道：“他应该如何爱你——或者他认为你有多聪明……？”过去的解释者将这首“十四行诗——科学”读为对客观调查的祛魅效果的标准浪漫攻击，然后在这个年轻的共和国中成长起来。在一个被科学理性的眼睛剖析并归结为机制的世界中，诗人的领域，奇迹和神秘如何生存？但正如科学史学家约翰·特雷施在他关于坡的新传记“夜之黑暗的原因”中所说的那样，还有另一种方式来阅读这首诗，甚至是坡，他深受喜爱的幻想故事影响了保罗·瓦莱里和约瑟夫·康拉德致斯蒂芬·金和乔丹·皮尔：Tresch 写道，这首诗远非对科学的攻击，而是可以理解为 Poe 呼吁将诗人和科学家的项目联合起来，“忠于科学的特定智慧形式，而不被摧毁”通过它。”追溯坡在动荡的生活中与科学的纠葛，其中许多被过去的传记作者所忽视，伦敦大学瓦尔堡研究所的教授特雷施提出了一个令人信服的案例，即与科学知识的后果作斗争是中心项目坡的作品。他还在坡的神秘故事和神秘诗句中发现了一种反对科学狂妄自大的良心，也是任何将科学视为想象力来源的人的榜样。在此过程中，特雷施 (Tresch) 提供了关于在战前美国围绕坡形成的疯狂理论、不道德的新闻和真正科学的令人兴奋的画像，所有这些都是这位神秘而怪异的大师的素材。正如坡所言：“科学日复一日地揭示的简单真理，事实上，显然比最疯狂的梦想要奇怪得多。”为了证明科学在坡的职业生涯中的中心地位，特雷施逐年跟踪坡，从他在弗吉尼亚州里士满的贵族成长经历，到他在巴尔的摩、纽约和费城充满敌意的文学圈中的崛起和最终垮台。在可能的情况下，当坡的生活与美国科学“锻造”中的关键事件和人物相交时，特雷施会绕道而行，特别关注史密森学会和美国科学促进会等科学机构的创建。结果是对狂欢节科学环境的全面（如果经常是切线）视图，坡在其中制作了一些他最喜爱和最被遗忘的作品，包括我们学习的关于贝壳的科学教科书。令人难以置信的是，《贝耳学家的第一本书，或者说，睾丸马拉色学系统》是坡一生中的畅销作品，其表现超过了《楠塔基特的亚瑟·戈登·皮姆的叙述》、《红死神的面具》等经典作品，甚至“乌鸦。” （其他历史学家指出，虽然坡——可能是因为他的受欢迎程度——被列为贝壳书的唯一作者，但他的角色更多是编辑；这本书本身最初是由科学家托马斯·怀亚特撰写的。）
但是这本书只是坡对科学的更明确的磨合之一。 Tresch 在 Poe 的工作和生活中发现了科学信号，几乎在他所看到的任何地方都能找到。坡出生于 1809 年（与查尔斯·达尔文同年），在与养父的紧张关系迫使他离开之前，他曾就读于以科学为导向的弗吉尼亚大学。他加入了美国陆军，特雷施指出当时美国陆军是“美国最可靠的技术知识机构”，在那里他负责监督弹药供应和设备，并创作了他的诗歌，包括一首受第谷布拉赫的天文观测启发的长诗。两年后，坡离开去西点军校谋取一份佣金，他获得了这份工作，但在不到一年的时间里又因为家庭闹剧而逃课。虽然相对简短，但特雷施认为，这种在西点军校期间接触技术和科学知识“决定性地塑造了 [坡] 作为诗人、评论家和作家的职业生涯”。 1831 年，坡离开西点军校后，失去了家人的经济支持，他前往巴尔的摩，在那里他靠卖作品勉强维持生计。回到里士满后，坡获得了《南方文学使者》编辑的职位，并开始以敏锐的批评家和挑衅作家的身份“修补美国文学体系的源泉和动力”。在他的余生中，坡一直在类似的编辑角色和其他出版计划之间徘徊，尽管他的名气越来越大，但为了实现财务和心理稳定而挣扎。 1849 年，他在巴尔的摩死于精神错乱——可能与酒精有关。当时他 40 岁。但在这段混乱的职业生涯中，特雷施找到了一条清晰的科学线索：坡发表了科普作品和技术手稿的评论，并参与了从星云形成到密码学等科学问题的争论。他还对当时猖獗的骗术进行了权衡，例如一位巡回表演者声称设计了一个下棋自动机。尽管特雷施指出坡挪用了他对国际象棋错觉的许多观察，但他在各个领域的着作使他成为“美国最早的科学记者之一”。尽管如此，坡本人也不能幸免于庸医，他发现像颅相学和动物磁性这样的时髦伪科学是完全合理的。 （尽管坡在这些问题上有大量的论述，但特雷施发现他在奴隶制问题和大多数时候其他紧迫的政治问题上基本上保持沉默——尽管作者还指出坡在 1829 年参与了贩卖一个被奴役的人。）坡也参与了他自己的一些恶作剧。有一次，利用 19 世纪的热气球热，他写了一篇关于在短短三天内横渡大西洋的气球之旅的记述。一个月后，他才透露这是一部小说——还有很多读者——后来。正如特雷施所解释的那样，坡明白，如果没有值得信赖的媒体和成熟的科学机构，“真理和信仰至少在一定程度上是风格问题。”
Tresch 展示了这些关于相信谁和相信什么科学主张的焦虑不仅激发了坡的写作，而且激发了美国一些最早的科学机构的创建：他们的精英创始人，坡在许多场合与他们擦肩，是越来越关心在什么是科学和什么仅仅是“骗子”之间划清界限——19 世纪的假新闻。 Tresch 是一位令人回味的亲密读者，他也从坡的小说的各个层面找到了科学信号：科学主题经常出现在坡的许多情节中，如小说“皮姆”中，关于“南大洋”的发现之旅。科学语言出现在坡对真实和超自然物理现象的精确描述中，如“厄舍之家的倒塌”中可怕的月光高潮。在坡的著名侦探人物 C. Auguste Dupin 所采用的归纳推理中，科学逻辑是显而易见的；甚至在他的诗句复杂的、像齿轮一样的结构中也是如此。坡对科学的参与和特雷施的书都在坡的生命即将结束时在纽约市发表的宇宙学讲座中达到高潮。在题为“论宇宙的宇宙学”（后来出版为“尤里卡”）的讲座中，坡提出了他对宇宙创造的看法。在接受科学事实时，坡描述了一个“原始粒子”爆炸成碎片，每个碎片都保留着“统一的欲望”。他解释说，吸引力和排斥力导致碎片化的物质形成星云，最终会在再次开始宇宙循环之前一起坍缩成“球体”。 Tresch 对这部密集的作品进行了令人钦佩的总结，他称其为“令人痛心的离题和不平衡”和“一团糟”。但也是“19 世纪美国科学与美学最有创意、最大胆、最独特的综合体之一”。在本书中更有趣的部分之一，不幸的是在结论中留下了几页，特雷施还追溯了“尤里卡”对现代宇宙学的可能影响，找到了坡与几位参与大爆炸和大爆炸理论的 20 世纪物理学家之间的联系。大紧缩，坡的“原始粒子”和“地球仪”似乎都在预料之中。特雷施还在“尤里卡”的一小段中找到了他的头衔，正如当代物理学家所承认的那样，坡成为第一个准确解释夜晚黑暗原因的人：宇宙中的恒星数量是有限的，因此它们之间存在暗空间。特雷施不是第一个评论坡与科学的许多联系的人，但他的新传记成功地给出了全面的观点，结合了对 19 世纪早期主要科学思想的充分了解，以及对坡大量科学思想的灵巧而经常深刻的阅读。工作。尽管有时很笨重，但对于那些被科学内外的奇异之美所吸引的人来说，这本书是有价值的阅读，并且像坡一样想知道诗人必须扮演什么角色。
note_en:
Edgar Allan Poe (1809-1849) American writer.							 (Universal History Archive/Getty Images)   "S CIENCE!..." opens a poem started in the late 1820s by a youthful Edgar Allan Poe and revised throughout his life. "Why prey'st thou upon the poet's heart, Vulture! Whose wings are dull realities!" Poe, a soon-to-be West Point cadet studying mathematics, geometry, and engineering, goes on mournfully: "How should he love thee — or how deem thee wise…?"
 Past interpreters have read this "Sonnet — To Science" as a standard romantic attack on the disenchanting effects of objective inquiry, then a growing force in the young republic. In a world dissected and reduced to mechanisms by the rational eye of science, how are wonder and mystery, the provinces of the poet, to survive?
 But as historian of science John Tresch argues in his new biography of Poe,  "The Reason for the Darkness of the Night," there's another way to read the poem and indeed Poe, whose beloved phantasmagorical tales have influenced writers from Paul Valéry and Joseph Conrad to Stephen King and Jordan Peele: Far from an attack on science, writes Tresch, the poem might be read as Poe's call to unite the projects of the poet and the scientist, to "stay true to science's specific form of wisdom, without being destroyed by it."
 Tracing the entanglements Poe had with science through his turbulent life, many of them overlooked by past biographers, Tresch, a professor at the Warburg Institute at the University of London, presents a convincing case that grappling with the consequences of scientific knowledge was the central project of Poe's work. He also finds in Poe's enigmatic stories and mystical verse a kind of conscience against scientific hubris, and a model for anyone who looks to science as a source of imaginative power.
 Along the way, Tresch offers a heady portrait of the crackpot theories, unscrupulous press, and genuine science taking shape around Poe in antebellum America, all of it material for this master of the uncanny and the weird. As quoth Poe: "The simple truths which science unfolds, day after day, are in fact, far stranger, apparently, than the wildest dreams."
 To make his case for the centrality of science in Poe's career, Tresch follows Poe year by year from his aristocratic upbringing in Richmond, Virginia through his rise and eventual downfall in the rancorous literary circles of Baltimore, New York, and Philadelphia. Wherever possible, Tresch takes detours when Poe's life intersects with key events and characters in the "forging" of American science, focusing especially on the creation of scientific institutions like the Smithsonian Institution and the American Association for the Advancement of Science.
 The result is a sweeping, if often tangential, view of the carnivalesque scientific milieu in which Poe crafted some of his most beloved and most forgotten works, including, we learn, a scientific textbook about shells. Incredibly, "The Conchologist's First Book, Or, A System Of Testaceous Malacology," was Poe's bestselling work during his life, outperforming classics like "The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket," "The Masque of the Red Death," and even "The Raven." (Other historians  have pointed out that while Poe — likely due to his popularity — was listed as the sole author of the shell book, his role was more editorial; the book itself was  originally written by the scientist Thomas Wyatt.)
 But the shell book was just one of Poe's more explicit run-ins with science. Tresch picks up the scientific signal across Poe's work and life, finding it nearly everywhere he looks.
 Born in 1809 (the same year as Charles Darwin), Poe studied at the science-oriented University of Virginia before tensions with his foster father forced him to leave. He joined the U.S. Army, which Tresch points out was then "the nation's most reliable body for technical knowledge," where he oversaw ammunition supplies and equipment, and worked on his poetry, including a long  poem inspired by the astronomical observations of Tycho Brahe.
 After two years, Poe left to seek a commission at West Point, which he gained, only to be thrown out in under a year for skipping class, again due to family drama. Though relatively brief, Tresch argues that this exposure to technical and scientific knowledge while at West Point "decisively shaped [Poe's] career as a poet, critic, and author."
 Out of West Point, and cut off from his family's financial support, Poe went to Baltimore in 1831, where he struggled to make ends meet selling his writing. After returning to Richmond, Poe secured a position as editor of the Southern Literary Messenger and began to build his name as an incisive critic and provocative author "tinkering with the springs and motive powers of the American system of literature." For the rest of his life, Poe would zigzag between similar editorial roles and other publishing schemes, struggling despite his growing fame to achieve financial and mental stability. In 1849, he died in a delirium —  possibly related to alcohol — in Baltimore. He was 40.
 But running through this chaotic career, Tresch traces a clear scientific thread: Poe published reviews of works of popular science and technical manuscripts, and engaged in disputes on scientific issues ranging from the formation of nebulae to cryptology. He also weighed in on the rampant quackery of the time, such as a traveling showman's claim to have designed a chess-playing automaton.
 Though Tresch notes that Poe appropriated many of his observations about the chess illusion, his writings on various fields made him "one of America's first science reporters." Still, Poe wasn't immune to quackery himself, finding trendy pseudosciences like phrenology and animal magnetism perfectly sensible. (Despite Poe's volume on these matters, Tresch finds him largely silent on the issue of slavery and most of the days other urgent political questions — though the author also notes Poe's involvement in the sale of an enslaved person in 1829.)
 Poe also participated in a few hoaxes of his own. Once, tapping into 19th century ballooning fever, he wrote an account of a balloon trip that had crossed the Atlantic in just three days. He only revealed it to be a fiction a month — and many readers — later. As Tresch explains, Poe understood that without a trustworthy press and established scientific institutions, "truth and belief were, at least in part, questions of style."
 Tresch shows how these anxieties over who and what to believe in a maelstrom of scientific claims inspired not only Poe's writing, but the creation of some of America's earliest scientific institutions: Their elite founders, with whom Poe rubbed elbows on a number of occasions, were increasingly concerned with drawing the line between what was science and what was merely "humbug" — the 19th century equivalent of fake news.
 Tresch, an evocative close reader, also picks up the scientific signal at all levels of Poe's fiction: Scientific subjects regularly appear in many of Poe's plots, as in the novel "Pym," about a journey of discovery to the "Southern Ocean." Scientific language appears in Poe's precise descriptions of physical phenomena, both real and supernatural, as in the horrifying moonlit climax of "The Fall of the House of Usher." And scientific logic is apparent in the inductive reasoning employed by Poe's famous detective character C. Auguste Dupin; it's even in the intricate, cog-like constructions of his verse.
 Both Poe's engagement with science and Tresch's book culminate in a cosmological lecture delivered in New York City near the end of Poe's life. In the lecture — titled "On the Cosmography of the Universe," later published as "Eureka" — Poe presented his view of the creation of the universe. Accepting scientific facts "as far as they went" at the time, Poe described a "primordial Particle" exploding into fragmented bits, each retaining an "appetite for unity." He explained forces of attraction and repulsion caused the fragmented matter to form nebulae, which would eventually collapse together into a "globe of globes" before beginning the cosmological cycle again.
 The dense work is admirably summarized by Tresch, who calls it "punishingly digressive and lopsided" and "a glorious mess." But also "one of the most creative, audacious, and idiosyncratic syntheses of science and aesthetics in nineteenth-century America." In one of the more intriguing parts of the book, unfortunately left to a few pages in the conclusion, Tresch also traces possible influences of "Eureka" on modern cosmology, finding connections between Poe and several 20th century physicists involved in theorizing the Big Bang and the Big Crunch, which Poe's "primordial Particle" and "globe of globes" both seem to anticipate.
 Tresch also finds his title in a short section of "Eureka," where Poe, as has been  acknowledged by contemporary physicists, became the first to accurately explain the reason for the darkness of the night: There are a finite number of stars in the universe, thus there are dark spaces between them.
 Tresch is not the first to comment on Poe's many connections with science, but his new biography succeeds in giving the full view, combining an ample tour through the major scientific ideas of the early 19th century with deft and frequently profound readings of Poe's large body of work. Though at times ponderous, the book is valuable reading for anyone drawn to the strange beauty within and just beyond science, and who wonders, as Poe did, what role a poet has to play.
title: 埃德加·爱伦·坡与美国科学界的互动
title_en: Edgar Allan Poe's engagement with American science

“温柔地对待她”：以色列军事搜索队如何帮助恢复大部分 Surfside 受害者 以色列国防军国家救援队从 Surfside 的 Champlain Towers South 公寓倒塌现场恢复了 97 名受害者中的 81 人。当以色列国防军国家救援队指挥官 Golan Vach 中校在瑟夫赛德 (Surfside) 倒塌的尚普兰塔南 (Champlain Towers South) 的废墟中搜寻时，发现了一枚男子戒指，他立即知道它属于谁。他知道，因为他见过数十名失踪者的家人。他研究了失踪人员的照片，了解了他们的财产——一条带心形吊坠的项链、一块金表、一张蓝色桌子。他了解了每个潜在受害者的习惯，甚至知道在崩溃期间每个人可能会在哪个房间。而且，在从曾经是他们家的混凝土海洋中伸出的难以想象的钢筋缠结中，他准确地知道每个受害者的位置。他亲自找到了 20 个。他说，当最后一名成员于 7 月 11 日离开 Surfside 时，他的团队找到了 81 个。总的来说，以色列团队找到了近 100 名遇难者中的 84%。
“这就是我们的工作方式，就像侦探一样，”Vach 说。 “你有这些愚蠢、简单、辉煌的迹象。你在这里挖掘，你会找到你要找的人。它奏效了。”在位于以色列北部加利利海以西约 15 英里的 Beit Rimon 家中进行的一系列独家、深入的电话、电子邮件和视频聊天中，Vach 描述了以色列国防军 (IDF) 所发挥的不可或缺的作用从部分倒塌的 13 层高、136 个单元的公寓大楼中救出受害者。碎石到未经训练的眼睛，每一块都是拼图的一部分，导致受害者 未经训练的眼睛看到的是一堆瓦砾，IDF 团队将其视为拼图形式的建筑物。每一块，无论是石柱、混凝土板，还是被压碎的传家宝，都将它们指向可能找到受害者的地方。可悲的是，他们的成功不会导致任何人的获救，而是为悲伤的亲人带来结束和安慰，并为他们提供机会说再见并开始治愈他们破碎的心。 Vach 讲述了如何在获得美国批准加入救援工作的几个小时内，他的团队登上了飞往迈阿密的商业航班。 Vach 说，飞机上有他的代表团的 15 名“最优秀的人”。他们于 6 月 27 日星期日早上 8 点抵达，也就是救援工作的第四天早上。他说，虽然他们计划到中午才开始工作，但团队直接前往坍塌现场。
“这就像一场比赛，大多数跑步者提前几秒钟开始，而你正试图赶上他们，”他说。 “72 小时后，我们不得不非常紧张、非常努力地工作，24/7 全天候在现场，试图找出是否还有活人的迹象。”他说，Vach 做的第一件事就是索取一份失踪人员名单。但他说当局告诉他他们不能发布这些信息。知道家人提供的精确细致的细节对救援工作至关重要，Vach 的团队成员立即开始寻找父母、兄弟姐妹、成年子女、表兄弟和其他亲人的任务，这些亲人因救援工作而苦苦等待。 “我从经验中知道，在这类事件中，每个小时都是昂贵的，”Vach 说。他们发现许多家庭住在以色列国防军所在的同一家酒店。他回忆说，仅仅过了六到八个小时，他的一名团队成员就来找他，她确定了他们应该寻找的人数。但这个数字与美国当局公布的数字不符。 “我说，'但官方数字是 159。你怎么只有 98？'”Vach 说。 “她说，'就是这样。不会超过 98 或 99 人。不会再有一个人了。'”迈阿密-戴德警方报告说，截至周五，已经确定了 97 名受害者，另有一人被归类为“可能失踪”。
Vach 说，Vach 的一半团队会见了受害者家属，另一半则部署到瓦砾堆中。在那里，他们发现急救人员在搜查犬吠叫的区域进行挖掘。 Vach 说，基于丰富的经验，他认为救援人员需要“更广泛的方法来尝试评估人们被埋葬在现场的确切位置。”他说，他的团队没有使用狗，而是使用了一种经过近 40 年磨练的公式。它是从以色列和世界各地（包括黎巴嫩、塞浦路斯、海地、日本、墨西哥、埃及、洪都拉斯和印度）的战争和灾难性事件中的响应任务中获得的专业知识。在 2005 年卡特里娜飓风淹没并摧毁新奥尔良时，该团队甚至随时提供帮助。 因此，就像试图解决最可怕的难题一样，Vach 的团队将其广泛的知识倾注到研究混凝土、钢筋和各种建筑材料以及受害者的消费品和个人财产。该团队还积累了 Vach 所谓的“现场救援情报”，其中包括与其他急救人员合作。他们确定了楼层之间的分界线，确定了柱子与单元相交的地方以及走廊曾经将邻居与邻居联系在一起的地方。在以色列团队的帮助下，Vach 的团队开发了 2-D 和 3-D 计算机模型，以确定建筑物是如何倒塌的，以及哪里可能存在可以支撑生命的“空隙”或裂缝。
结果是一系列数字图像精确显示了每个房间在倒塌前后的位置：红色代表卧室，蓝色代表餐厅，绿色代表厨房，黄色代表走廊。暗红色三角形标记了应该找到受害者的位置。团队在堆中标记了每一列、每个房间、每一层。橙色喷漆和红砖——那是九楼。蓝色喷漆将整个碎片场隔离成一个网格。该团队创造了一个“脆弱的现实”，计算并绘制了一个受害者可能与另一个受害者之间的距离。这样，救援人员可以有目的地搜索，测量距离和方向——向西 13 英尺，向南 33½ 英尺，向东 29 英尺——从每个受害者到可能找到另一个受害者的地方。就这样继续细致地绘​​制网站图表。随着每个人或个人物品的恢复和识别，团队确认或修改了他们的模型。 “他们发现的每个项目都是纠正计划的锚点，”Vach 说。 “如果我假设我在 404 号公寓的 4 楼，但我发现了一张蓝色桌子，并且我知道这张蓝色桌子属于公寓 3，而不是 4 号公寓，那么有什么地方不正确，有什么东西移动了。我必须纠正我的计划。”他说，有一次，另一名救援人员发现了一只死去的狗，正准备丢弃它。 “我说，”不，不要停下来！'”Vach 说。 “看看这座建筑——据我们所知，只有两只狗。一只是斗牛犬，一只是一只大灰狗。每只狗都属于不同的公寓。如果我们确切地知道你在哪里找到这只狗，我们就可以尝试纠正我们的计划。”
瓦赫似乎知道尚普兰塔南的每一个单元号，每一个居民。在提到任何一个时，他都可以识别另一个。 1104 单元，Itty 和 Tzvi Ainsworth。 7 岁的父母，30 岁的祖父母。“当我和 Ainsworth 一家坐在一起时，他们是我唯一详细描述了我如何找到他们、提取他们、将他们拉出来的家庭，”他说。 “这并不容易。” Vach 找到了 Stella Cattarossi，她是迈阿密戴德市一名消防员的 7 岁女儿，她也是搜救队的一员。这个孩子靠近住在 501 单元的她的母亲和祖父母的尸体。 Vach 说，在他的团队的帮助下，在以色列的团队的帮助下找到了另外两名受害者，他发现了一块写着“拉比 AD 戴维斯”的小牌匾。 Vach 回忆说，当时是凌晨 4 点左右，在 20 分钟内，他的团队找到了拉比，当时他不在大楼里，但告诉他们他知道大楼里有一个人。瓦赫说，拉比将牌匾作为礼物送给了住在 1110 单元的布拉德·科恩。“所以我知道我和布拉德很亲近，”瓦赫说他当时想。 7 月 7 日，瓦赫找到了科恩。他还找到了科恩的兄弟加里，他从城外来访。 “你看到了很多死亡，”瓦赫说。 “有些看起来不像人类。我试着把他们当作人，当作灵魂。像对待我的父母一样对待他们。”
尽管如此，找到心爱之人的心碎从未变得如此容易，Vach 轻声说道，他的一些团队真的很挣扎。 “我对他们说，‘嘿，没关系。她等了我们五天才被找到。温柔地对待她。用毯子盖住她。总有一天，你会是一样的，'”他说。以色列总领事 Maor Elbaz Starinsky 表示，严酷的现实是，以色列团队对他们所做的事情如此精通，因为他们有很多机会磨练自己的技能。 “不幸的是，我们在灾难方面有很多经验，”他说。 “以色列城镇和人口中心已成为中东战区的新阵线。 ......因此，我们的军队和当局开发了非常先进的技术，可用于战区以外的地区。”他在以色列家中接受电话采访时说，虽然斯塔林斯基已经在外交部工作了 15 年，但当尚普兰塔南倒塌时，他担任以色列驻迈阿密总领事的新职位仅四天。他说，由于以色列政府的动荡，他在几个月前最初接受该职位后尚未得到确认。
“对于整个任务来说，这是一个非常坎坷的开始，”他说，并补充说以色列外交部长在崩溃后立即指示他“不遗余力”提供协助。 “对我们来说，最自然的事情仍然是，永远都是，向我们最大的盟友提供帮助，”他说。 “以色列永远不会有比美国更好的朋友，美国也永远不会有比以色列更好的盟友或朋友。”斯塔林斯基说，他提供了他的个人信用卡，以帮助那些在崩溃中幸存但失去所有财产的人开处方，并购买毯子、鞋子和食物。他说，他的团队还帮助失踪人员的家人安排了从以色列、委内瑞拉和哥伦比亚等地出发的旅行，当他们抵达迈阿密时，以色列驻华盛顿大使馆为他们提供了进一步帮助。 “这不是犹太人的悲剧，这是人类的悲剧，而是国际的悲剧，”他说。斯塔林斯基说，虽然美军的数量远远超过以色列军队，但以色列军队已经学会了事半功倍。他说：“我们在运营业务的方式上必须非常、非常明智和聪明。” “我们已经学会了成为我们所说的‘小而快’。”
他说，美国和以色列的团队完美地互补，每个团队都利用各自最大的优势尽一切可能从他所谓的“非常戏剧性”、“暴力”的崩溃中营救和恢复受害者。虽然有些人质疑如果以色列国防军团队早点加入，是否可以挽救更多生命，但斯塔林斯基并不确定。 “也许，也许，也许，”他慢慢地说，思考着这个问题。 “我们花了几天时间来证明我们的独特性和效率；为什么值得让我们从半个地球过来帮助美国？”不过，在考虑之后，他说他认为这不会有太大的不同。“可能是因为这件事的性质他说：“我不确定我们是否更早进行了协调，这是否会改变这场灾难的结果。”他说，造成这种情况的主要原因是建筑物倒塌的方式如此不寻常。“当建筑物的一部分被破坏或倒塌时，它不像地震或城市导弹那样，你有很多空隙、洞和隧道他说：“这栋建筑几乎像预期的那样倒塌。当他们内爆另一部分时，它内爆成完美的形状。”
然后，他停顿了片刻，显然被记忆所震撼，然后几乎是在他的呼吸中补充道，“说'完美'这个词真是太糟糕了。”

'Treat her gently': How an Israeli military search team helped recover majority of Surfside victims
 The Israeli Defense Forces’ National Rescue Unit recovered 81 of the 97 victims from the Champlain Towers South condo collapse site in Surfside.
     When Lt. Col. Golan Vach, commander of the Israeli Defense Forces’ National Rescue Unit, located a man’s ring while searching the debris field of the collapsed Champlain Towers South in Surfside, he knew immediately to whom it belonged.
 He knew, because he had met with families of the scores of missing people. He had studied photographs of the missing, learned about  their possessions — a necklace with a heart pendant, a gold watch, a blue table.
 He had learned about the habits of each potential victim and even knew in which room each likely would have been during the collapse.
 And, amid unimaginable tangles of rebar jutting out from a sea of concrete that was once their homes, he knew precisely where each victim would be located.
  He personally found 20. His team, he said, recovered 81 by the time the last member departed Surfside on July 11. In all, the Israeli team found about 84% of the nearly 100 victims.
 “This is the way we were working, like detectives,” Vach said. “You have these stupid, simple, brilliant signs. You dig here, and you will find the people you are looking for. And it worked.”
     In a series of exclusive, in-depth phone calls, emails and video chats from his home in Beit Rimon — located about 15 miles west of the Sea of Galilee in northern Israel — Vach described the integral role the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) played in recovering victims from the partially collapsed, 13-story, 136-unit condominium complex.
  Rubble to untrained eye, each piece was part of a puzzle that led to victims
 What the untrained eye saw as a pile of rubble the IDF team viewed as a building in puzzle form. And each piece, whether a stone column, slab of concrete, or crushed family heirloom, pointed them toward where a victim might be found.
 Sadly, their success would not result in anyone's rescue, but rather in bringing closure and solace to grieving loved ones and providing them an opportunity to say goodbye and begin to heal their broken hearts.
  Vach related how,  within hours of receiving approval from the U.S. to join the rescue efforts, his team boarded a commercial flight to Miami.
 On the plane were 15 of his delegation's “best people,” Vach said. They arrived at 8 a.m. on Sunday, June 27, the morning of the fourth day of rescue efforts. And while they were not scheduled to begin work until noon, he said, the team headed straight to the collapse site.
  “It’s like a race where most of the runners began a few seconds ahead and you are trying to catch them,” he said. “After 72 hours, we had to work very tight, very hard, to be onsite 24/7 to try to find out if there are still signs of a living person.”
 The first thing Vach did, he said, was request a list of missing persons. But he said authorities told him they could not release that information.
   Knowing precise and meticulous details provided by family members would be crucial to rescue efforts, members of Vach’s team immediately began the task of searching out parents, siblings, adult children, cousins and other loved ones stuck in agonizing wait for word on rescue efforts.
 "I know from experience that each hour is expensive in those kinds of incidents," Vach said.
 They found many families staying in the same hotel where the IDF team was housed. In only six to eight hours, he recalled, one of his team members came to him with what she determined would be the number of people for whom they should be searching. But that number didn’t match the one put out by U.S. authorities.
  “I said, ‘But the official numbers are 159. How come you have only 98?’” Vach said. “And she said, ‘This is it. There will be no more than 98 or 99. Not a single one more.’”
 As of Friday, 97 victims had been identified, reported Miami-Dade Police, with one more categorized as “potentially missing.”
    While half of Vach’s team met with victims’ families, the other half deployed to the rubble pile, Vach said. There, they found first responders digging in areas where search dogs were barking.
 Based on vast experience, Vach said, he believed the rescuers would need a “much wider methodology to try to evaluate where exactly people were buried in the site.”
  His team did not use dogs, he said, but rather a formula that had been honed over almost 40 years. It was expertise developed from responder missions in wars and catastrophic events in Israel and around the world, including in Lebanon, Cyprus, Haiti, Japan, Mexico, Egypt, Honduras and India. The team had even been on hand to assist during Hurricane Katrina when it flooded and devastated New Orleans in 2005.
  So, like attempting to solve the most gruesome of puzzles, Vach’s team poured its sweeping knowledge into studying the pile of concrete, rebar and assorted building materials along with victims' consumer goods and personal possessions.
 The team also amassed what Vach called "field rescue intelligence," which included collaborating with other first responders.
 They identified dividing lines between floors, determined where columns intersected units and where hallways once linked neighbor to neighbor.
  With help from his team back in Israel, Vach's group developed 2-D and 3-D computer models to determine how the building had fallen and where there might be “voids'' or crevices that could support life.
 The result was a series of digital images that showed precisely where each room was located both before and after the collapse: red for bedrooms, blue for dining rooms, green for kitchens, yellow for corridors. A dark red triangle marked the spot where a victim should be found.
  The team marked each column, each room, each floor in the pile. Orange spray paint and red brick — that was the ninth floor. Blue spray paint segregated the entire debris field into a grid.
 The team created a “fragile reality,” calculating and mapping how far one victim might have landed from another. That way, rescuers could search with purpose, measuring out the distance and direction — 13 feet to the west, 33½ feet to the south, 29 feet to the east — from each victim to where another might be found.
  And so continued the meticulous process of diagramming the site. With each person or personal item recovered and identified, the team confirmed or modified their model.
 “Each item that they found is an anchor to correct the plan,” Vach said. “If I assume I am on Floor 4 in apartment 404, but I found a blue table, and I know the blue table belonged to Apartment 3, not 4, then something is incorrect, something moved. And I have to correct my plan.”
  Once, he said, another rescuer found a deceased dog and was about to discard it.
 “I said, “No, no stop!'” Vach said. “Look at this building — according to what we know there were only two dogs. One was a pit bull and one was a big gray dog. Each dog belonged to a different apartment. If we know exactly where you found this dog, we can try to correct our plan.”
  Vach seemed to know every unit number, every resident of Champlain Towers South. At the mention of either, he can identify the other. Unit 1104,  Itty and Tzvi Ainsworth. Parents to seven, grandparents to 30.
 “When I sat with the Ainsworth family, they were the only family that I described in detail how I found them, extracted them, pulled them out,” he said. “It’s not easy.”
  Vach recovered Stella Cattarossi, the 7-year-old daughter of a Miami-Dade firefighter who was also part of the search-and-rescue team. The child was near the body of her mother and grandparents, who lived in unit 501.
  Two other victims were located with the help of his team back in Israel, Vach said, after he found a tiny plaque that read “Rabbi A. D. Davis.” It was around 4 a.m., Vach recalled, and within 20 minutes, his team had located the rabbi, who was not in the building at the time but told them he knew of a person in the building.
  Vach said the rabbi had given the plaque as a gift to Brad Cohen, who lived in unit 1110. "So I know I am close to Brad," Vach said he thought at the time.
 On July 7, Vach found Cohen. He also found Cohen’s brother, Gary, who was visiting from out of town.
 “You see a lot of death,” Vach said. “Some do not look like human beings. I tried to treat them as people, as souls. Treat them as if they were my parents.”
 Still, the heartbreak of finding someone’s loved one never gets easier, Vach said softly, and some of his team really struggled.
 “I say to them, ‘Hey, it's OK. She was waiting for us for five days to be found. Treat her gently. Cover her with a blanket. One day, you will be the same,’” he said.
   The harsh reality, said Maor Elbaz Starinsky, the consul general of Israel, is that the Israeli teams are so proficient at what they do because they have had so much opportunity to sharpen their skills.
 “Unfortunately, we have a lot of experience with disasters,” he said. “Israeli towns and cities and population centers have become the new front for the war zone in the Middle East. ...
 So our military and authorities have developed very advanced techniques that are useful in territories other than war zones.”
  While Starinsky already had 15 years' experience in the foreign ministry, he was only four days into his new position as Israeli consul general in Miami when Champlain Towers South collapsed, he said in a telephone interview from his home in Israel.
 He said he had not been confirmed after initially accepting the position months earlier because of turmoil in the Israeli government.
  “It was a really bumpy start for this whole mission,” he said, adding that the Israeli foreign minister immediately after the collapse instructed him to “spare no effort” in assisting.
 “For us, the most natural thing still is, and forever will be, to offer our help to our biggest ally,” he said. “Israel will never have a better friend than the U.S., and the U.S. will never have a better ally or friend than Israel.”
   Starinsky said he provided his personal credit card to help fill prescriptions and buy blankets, shoes and food for those who had survived the collapse but lost all of their possessions.
 His team also helped family members of the missing arrange travel from places such as Israel, Venezuela and Colombia, he said, and the Israeli Embassy in Washington helped them even further when they arrived in Miami.
  "This is not a Jewish tragedy, this is a human tragedy, it’s an international tragedy," he said.
 Starinsky said that while U.S. forces vastly outnumber Israeli forces, Israeli forces have learned to do more with less.
 "We have to be very, very wise and clever in the way we run our operations," he said. "We have learned to be what we call 'small and swift.'"
  U.S. and Israeli teams complemented one another beautifully, he said, each drawing on their greatest strengths to do everything possible to rescue and recover victims from what he called a “very dramatic,” “violent” collapse.
  And while some have questioned if more lives could have been saved if the IDF team had been brought in sooner, Starinsky is not so sure.
 “Maybe, maybe, maybe,” he said slowly, contemplating the question. “It took us a few days to prove our uniqueness, our efficiency; why it was worth schlepping us, commuting us, from halfway across the world to help the U.S."
  Still, after thinking about it, he said he doesn't think it would have made much difference.
 “Probably, because of the nature of this collapse, everybody died instantly or very close to instantly,” he said. “I'm not sure if we had the coordination earlier it would change the result of this disaster.”
 The primary reason for that, he said, was the way the building collapsed was so unusual.
 “It was not like an earthquake or urban missiles when part of the building is broken or falls down and you have a lot of voids and holes and tunnels that are created,” he said. “This building collapsed almost as if it was intended. When they  imploded the other part, it imploded in the perfect shape.”
 Then, he paused for a moment, clearly shaken by the memory, before adding almost under his breath, “It's a terrible word to say, ‘perfect.’”
title: 以色列国防军恢复了 80% 的迈阿密坍塌遗迹

根据以色列卫生部的一份新报告，辉瑞和 BioNTech 的 Covid-19 疫苗在以色列的有效性仅为 39%，其中 delta 变体是主要毒株，但仍能提供强有力的保护，防止严重疾病和住院治疗。疗效数据基于 6 月 20 日至 7 月 17 日期间未指明人数的数据，低于两周前早期估计的 64%，并且与来自英国的数据相冲突，该数据表明该疫苗对有症状的患者有 88% 的有效性。变异引起的疾病。然而，根据以色列周四公布的数据，两剂疫苗在预防人们患重病方面仍然非常有效，对住院的有效率为 88%，对严重疾病的有效率为 91%。 “我们必须注意，随着时间的推移，这些疫苗的有效性可能会减弱，”多伦多大学传染病教授 Isaac Bogoch 博士说。他强调，这些疫苗在预防严重感染方面仍然非常有效，有助于医院系统在进入寒冷月份时不会过于不堪重负。话虽如此，“我们仍处于 Covid 时代，任何事情都有可能发生，”他说。 “我们必须做好准备，我们必须灵活应对人们可能在某个时候需要助推器，”他补充道。 “在以色列、英国和世界其他地区等国家进行的这种密切监视将非常有助于推动政策，如果我们确实需要助推器的话。” delta 变异已经在超过 104 个国家/地区出现，引起美国卫生官员的关注，因为他们看到更多的突破性感染发生在完全接种疫苗的人群中，尽管他们的症状更轻。
白宫首席医疗官安东尼·福奇博士说，完全接种疫苗的人可能会考虑在室内戴口罩，以预防这种病毒在美国迅速传播 健康专家担心秋季，预计 delta 将袭击疫情最严重的州疫苗接种率最高——除非这些州和企业重新引入口罩规定、容量限制和其他他们已经基本取消的公共卫生措施。 “这显然是我们不想看到的，”福奇周三说，并指出了所谓的突破性感染。 “这种病毒显然不同于我们之前接触过的病毒和变种。它具有非凡的人际传播能力。”为 FDA 提供 Covid 疫苗建议的 Paul Offit 博士说，虽然这些疫苗仍能提供极好的预防严重疾病和死亡的保护，但它们对轻度病例或将疾病传播给他人可能效果不佳。他敦促更多美国人接种疫苗，称 delta 是一种具有高度传染性的病毒，疫苗将帮助人们避免重病。根据疾病预防控制中心汇编的数据，目前只有不到一半的美国人口完全接种了疫苗。 “这是病毒继续自我繁殖并继续创造可能对疫苗或自然感染越来越有抵抗力的变种的肥沃土壤，”他说。世卫组织官员周一表示，世界各地的人们未接种疫苗和社会融合持续的时间越长，出现更危险变异的风险就越高。
以色列的报告先于许多其他国家开始为其人口接种疫苗，这可能会支持制药商的论点，即人们最终需要接种加强疫苗以预防新出现的变异。辉瑞本月早些时候表示，它开始看到其两剂疫苗的免疫力下降，现在计划寻求食品和药物管理局的授权以获得加强剂量。然而，联邦官员表示，完全接种疫苗的美国人此时不需要额外注射。辉瑞在给 CNBC 的一份声明中表示，它仍然相信其两剂方案可以预防冠状病毒及其变种。尽管如此，它表示，在其第三阶段研究的分析显示，在四到六个月后，对有症状感染的疗效下降后，第三剂可能会有所帮助。 “当前疫苗第三剂的初始数据表明，在第二剂接种后至少 6 个月给予加强剂量会引起对野生型和 Beta 的高中和效价，比两次主要剂量后高 5 到 10 倍， “该公司表示。
note_en:
Pfizer and  BioNTech's Covid-19 vaccine is just 39% effective in Israel where the delta variant is the dominant strain, but still provides strong protection against severe illness and hospitalization, according to a new report from the country's Health Ministry.
 The efficacy figure, which is based on an unspecified number of people between June 20 and July 17, is down from an earlier estimate of  64% two weeks ago and conflicts with data out of the U.K. that found the shot was 88% effective against symptomatic disease caused by the variant.
  However, the two-dose vaccine still works very well in preventing people from getting seriously sick, demonstrating 88% effectiveness against hospitalization and 91% effectiveness against severe illness, according to the Israeli data published Thursday.
 "We have to be mindful that, with time, the effectiveness of these vaccines may wane," said Dr. Isaac Bogoch, an infectious disease professor at the University of Toronto.
  He stressed that the shots are still highly effective in preventing severe infection, helping hospital systems not get too overwhelmed heading into the colder months. That being said, "we're still in the Covid era and anything can happen," he said.
 "We have to be prepared and we have to be nimble that people may need a booster at some point," he added. "This close surveillance that's happening in countries like Israel, the U.K. and other parts of the world is going to be very helpful in driving policy if and when we do need boosters."
 The delta variant, already in more than 104 countries, is concerning health officials in the U.S. as they see more breakthrough infections, which occur in fully vaccinated people, even though they are more mild.
  White House chief medical officer Dr. Anthony Fauci said fully vaccinated people might want to consider wearing masks indoors as a precaution against the rapidly spreading variant in the U.S.
 Health experts are  concerned about the fall season, when delta is expected to hit states with the lowest vaccination rates the hardest — unless those states and businesses reintroduce mask rules, capacity limits and other public health measures that they've largely rolled back.
 "That's something we obviously don't want to see," Fauci  said Wednesday, noting the so-called breakthrough infections. "This virus is clearly different than the viruses and the variants that we've had experience with before. It has an extraordinary capability of transmitting from person to person."
 Dr. Paul Offit, who advises the FDA on Covid vaccines, said while the vaccines still provide excellent protection against severe disease and death, they may not work as well against mild cases or spreading the disease to others.
 He urged more Americans to get vaccinated, saying delta is a highly contagious virus and the shots will help people from getting seriously sick. Currently, less than half of the U.S. population is fully vaccinated, according to data compiled by the CDC.
 "That is a rich and fertile ground for the virus to continue to reproduce itself and continue to create variants that possibly become more and more resistant to vaccines or natural infection," he said.
 WHO officials said Monday that the longer that people around the world remain unvaccinated and social mixing continues, the higher the risk of a more dangerous variant to emerge.
 The report out of Israel, which began vaccinating its population ahead of many other countries, is likely to bolster arguments from drugmakers that people will eventually need to get booster shots to protect against emerging variants.
 Pfizer  said earlier this month it is starting to see waning immunity from its two-dose vaccine, and now plans to seek authorization from the Food and Drug Administration for a booster dose. However, federal officials say fully vaccinated Americans do not need additional shots at this time.
 In a statement to CNBC, Pfizer said it remains confident its two-dose regimen is protective against the coronavirus and its variants.
 Still, it said a third dose may be helpful after analysis from its phase three study showed a decline in efficacy against symptomatic infection after four to six months.
 "Initial data of a third dose of the current vaccine demonstrates that a booster dose given at least 6 months after the second dose elicits high neutralization titers against the wild type and the Beta, which are 5 to 10 times higher than after two primary doses," the company said.
title: 以色列表示，随着 delta 的蔓延，辉瑞 Covid 疫苗的有效性仅为 39%
title_en: Israel says Pfizer Covid vaccine is just 39% effective as delta spreads

note: 我的个人 Facebook 帐户已被错误停用，因为我试图在 FB 市场以及其他专注于棋盘游戏的 FB 群组中出售我使用过的棋盘游戏“秘密希特勒”的副本。我猜 FB 的算法根据棋盘游戏的名称标记了这一点，并禁用了我违反社区标准的帐户，尽管人们可以很容易地看出它没有上下文。这显然是一个错误，但在我对该决定提出上诉后，FB 并没有改变他们的决定，我也没有其他选择可以上诉。我完全不知道如何纠正这个问题，尤其令人沮丧，因为我的个人 FB 帐户不仅与我的 Oculus 库（我无法再访问）而且我的 FB 业务页面相关联。完全荒谬。
note_en: My personal facebook account has been deactivated mistakenly because I attempted to sell my used copy of the board game "Secret Hitler" in FB marketplace, among other boardgame focused FB groups. I am guessing FB's algorithms flagged this due to the name of the boardgame and disabled my account for violating the community standards although one can easily see that it is without context. This is obviously a mistake and yet after I appealed the decision, FB has not changed their decision and I have no other options to appeal. I am completely bewildered as to how to rectify this and it is particularly frustrating as my personal FB account is tied to not only my Oculus library (which I can no longer access) but also my FB business page. Completely absurd.

特斯拉首席执行官埃隆·马斯克表示，如果该公司能够为其储能产品获得足够的芯片，该部门的需求情况将变得更加乐观。特斯拉周一报告其能源生产和存储业务的收入为 8.01 亿美元，其中包括三个主要产品：太阳能、家庭和企业的 Powerwall 存储设备，以及其公用事业存储部门 Megapack，但这只是近 120 亿美元的一小部分在总收入中。尽管规模很小，但该部门正在销售更多的储能和太阳能。该部门的收入比上一季度增长 62%，比 2020 年同期增长 116% 以上。特斯拉没有将太阳能和储能收入分开。更重要的是，其太阳能和储能业务的收入成本为 7.81 亿美元，这意味着生产和分销这些储能产品的总成本首次低于其产生的收入。那是好消息。正如人们所料，总部署量也有所增加。特斯拉在 2021 年第二季度安装了 1,274 兆瓦时的储能，比去年同期增长 205%。同样，今年第二季度部署的太阳能数量为 85 MWh，比 2020 年第二季度增长 214%。 附带说明：比较 2019 年第二季度和 2020 年第二季度的数据，特斯拉的太阳能和储能部署总量基本持平，可能是由于大流行导致业务普遍停止。重要的金块是收入增长。 2019 年，特斯拉报告的太阳能和存储收入为 3.69 亿美元。 2020 年第二季度的收入停滞不前，该业务的收入为 3.7 亿美元。本季度是特斯拉在 2019 年和 2020 年同期收入的两倍多。发生了什么变化？除了 COVID-19，特斯拉还指出了几个 Megapack 项目上线并在其太阳能和 Powerwall 组合产品中越来越受欢迎。 （特斯拉不再允许客户在没有安装太阳能的情况下订购 Powerwall。）根据特斯拉网站上的配置器，一个 Megapack 的税前价值约为 120 万美元。在一些州，特斯拉表示最早的交付时间是 2023 年。然而，特斯拉的储能业务正面临逆风。马斯克表示，对 Megapack 和 Powerwall 的需求都超过了供应量，而且积压的数量正在增加。他说，由于全球芯片短缺，该公司无法满足这种需求。
特斯拉在其 Powerwall 中使用与在其车辆中相同的芯片，马斯克表示，在供应不足的情况下，车辆是优先事项。马斯克在财报电话会议上表示：“随着严重短缺的缓解，我们可以大规模提高 Powerwall 的产量。” “我认为我们明年有可能达到 100 万台 Powerwall 的年化率——也许每周 20,000 台。再次取决于电池供应和半导体。 … 随着世界向可持续能源生产过渡，太阳能和风能是间歇性的，从本质上讲，它们确实需要电池组来提供稳定的电力流动。当你看看世界上所有的公用事业时，这是需要大量的备用电池。”马斯克表示，从长远来看，特斯拉和其他供应商每年总共需要生产 1,000 至 2,000 吉瓦时才能满足储能需求。马斯克表示，该公司已要求其电池供应商在 2022 年将供应量增加一倍，马斯克警告说，这一目标将取决于供应链问题。马斯克表示，该公司目前的战略是增加电池供应量，并将其用于储能产品，但在芯片短缺的情况下，将优先考虑汽车生产。虽然大部分电池讨论都集中在其正在开发的 4680 电池上，但马斯克还提到了特斯拉打算用更便宜的磷酸铁锂 (LFP) 电池为其部分产品供电的意图。具体而言，他表示，所有固定存储很有可能转向铁基电池，而不再使用镍锰钴 (NMC) 和镍钴铝电池。 “我认为可能会发生转变，我的猜测可能是三分之二的铁，三分之一的镍，”马斯克谈到特斯拉的计划时说。 “这实际上很好，因为世界上有相当多的铁，数量惊人的铁。但是镍的含量要少得多，钴的含量要少得多。”三分之一的镍基电池将用于长续航里程的电动汽车。它的所有其他电动汽车也将转向 LFP 电池，这在其在中国组装的汽车中已经如此。
note_en:
The demand picture will get even sunnier for the division if the company can access enough chips for its energy storage products, according to Tesla CEO Elon Musk.
 Tesla on Monday  reported $801 million in revenue from its energy generation and storage business — which includes three main products: solar, its Powerwall storage device for homes and businesses, and its utility storage unit Megapack — but that’s just a sliver of the nearly  $12 billion in total revenue. Small as it is, the division is selling more energy storage and solar. Revenue from this division grew 62% from the previous quarter and more than 116% from the same quarter in 2020. Tesla doesn’t separate solar and energy storage revenue.
 More importantly, the cost of revenue for its solar and energy storage business was $781 million, meaning that for the first time the total cost of producing and distributing these energy storage products was lower than the revenue it generated. That’s good news.
 As one might expect, total deployments also rose. Tesla installed 1,274 megawatt-hours of energy storage in the second quarter of 2021, a 205% increase from the same period last year. Similarly, the amount of solar energy deployed in the second quarter of this year was 85 MWh, up 214% from Q2 2020. As a side note: Tesla’s total solar and energy storage deployments were essentially flat when comparing Q2 2019 and Q2 2020 numbers, likely due to the pandemic’s general halting of business.
 The important nugget is revenue growth. In 2019, Tesla reported $369 million in revenue from solar and storage. Revenue was stagnant in Q2 2020, with $370 million from that business. This quarter was more than double what Tesla brought in during the same quarters of 2019 and 2020.
 What changed? Besides COVID-19, Tesla points to several Megapack projects coming online and growing popularity in its combined solar and Powerwall product. (Tesla no longer allows customers to order Powerwall without a solar installation.)  According to a configurator on Tesla’s website, one Megapack is about $1.2 million before taxes. In some states, Tesla says the earliest deliveries will be in 2023.
 Tesla’s energy storage business is facing headwinds, however. Musk said demand for both the Megapack and the Powerwall both exceed supply, and a backlog is growing. The company is unable to meet that demand because of the global chip shortage, he said.
 Tesla uses the same chips in its Powerwall as it does in its vehicles, and Musk said vehicles are the priority while supply is low.
 “As that significant shortage is alleviated, then we can massively ramp up Powerwall production,” Musk said during an earnings call. “I think we have a chance of hitting an annualized rate of a million units of Powerwall next year — maybe, on the order of 20,000 a week. Again dependent on cell supply and semiconductors. … As the world transitions to a sustainable energy production, solar and wind are intermittent, and by their nature really need battery packs in order to provide a steady flow of electricity. And when you look at all the utilities in the world, this is a vast amount of backup batteries that are needed.”
 Musk said in the long term, Tesla and other suppliers would need to produce a combined 1,000 to 2,000 gigawatt-hours per year in order to keep up with energy storage demands. Musk said the company has asked its cell suppliers to double their supply in 2022, a goal that Musk caveated would be dependent on supply chain issues. The company’s current strategy is to overshoot cell supply and route it outward to its energy storage products, but as in the case of chip shortages, vehicle production would be prioritized, according to Musk.
  While much of the battery cell discussion focused on its 4680 cell that is in development, Musk also touched on Tesla’s intentions to power some of its products with cheaper lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) batteries. Specifically, he said there’s a good chance that all stationary storage could move to iron-based batteries and away from nickel-manganese-cobalt (NMC) and nickel-cobalt-aluminum batteries.
 “I think probably will see a shift, my guess is probably to two-thirds iron, one-third nickel,” Musk said of Tesla’s plans. “And this is actually good because there’s quite a bit of iron in the world, an insane amount of iron. But there’s much less nickel and there’s way less cobalt.”
 The one-third of batteries that will remain nickel-based will be used for its longer-range electric vehicles. All of its other EVs would also move to LFP batteries, which is already the case in its vehicles assembled in China.
title: 特斯拉太阳能和储能业务收入 8.1 亿美元，最终超过收入成本
title_en: Tesla’s solar and energy storage business rakes in $810M, finally exceeds cost of revenue

note:
特斯拉首席执行官埃隆·马斯克之前曾就电池供应限制及其可能对特斯拉 Semi 产生的影响发出警告，特斯拉 Semi 于 2017 年 11 月首次作为原型亮相。早在 1 月份，马斯克就表示，特斯拉 Semi 的工程工作已经完成并预计今年开始交付。他当时确实补充说，电池的可用性可能会限制公司生产 Semi 的能力。这个警告显然是有道理的。摘自今天收盘后发布的股东信：我们相信我们仍有望在 2021 年在柏林和奥斯汀制造我们的第一辆 Model Y 汽车。各自的生产速度将受到许多新产品的成功推出的影响和制造技术、持续的供应链相关挑战和区域许可。为了更好地专注于这些工厂，由于电池供应有限和全球供应链挑战，我们将半卡车计划的启动时间推迟到 2022 年。我们也在推进 Cyber​​truck 的产业化，目前计划Model Y 之后的奥斯汀生产。 虽然在电话会议或收益报告中没有提到，但延迟是在特斯拉的关键高管杰罗姆·吉伦 (Jerome Guillen) 离职之后进行的，他正致力于特斯拉 Semi 的开发和最终生产。 Guillen 于 6 月辞职，仅仅三个月后，他就从汽车总裁职位（包括监督特斯拉 Semi 公司）调任重型卡车运输主管的职责较少的职位。从 2018 年 9 月到 2021 年 3 月，Guillen 一直领导着特斯拉的整个汽车业务。 与此同时，原定于 2021 年底投产的特斯拉 Cyber​​truck 看起来也可能会被推迟到明年。马斯克没有回答问题，但马斯克和特斯拉工程副总裁 Lars Moravy 在财报电话会议上的评论表明，它可能会转移到 2022 年。 Cyber​​truck 目前处于原型设计的 alpha 阶段，具有基本的工程和架构车辆完成。 Moravy 说，虽然 Model Y 优先，但该公司将在今年晚些时候进入 Cyber​​truck 的测试阶段。
“在 Model Y 启动并运行后，我们将寻求在德克萨斯州提高产量，”他补充道。马斯克认为 Cyber​​truck 的难度有多大，或许是为了缓和对 2021 年到来的预期。“Cyber​​truck 坡道将很困难，因为它是一种新架构，”马斯克说。 “这将是一个很棒的产品；它可能是我们有史以来最好的产品，但它确实有很多全新的设计理念。”他继续指出，当其他车辆从原型车转向批量生产时，他曾使用过的观点是：制造很难。 “冒着重复的风险，制作原型或少量小批量生产实际上很容易，但是任何大批量生产的东西，这真的很重要，它是否会像最慢的一样快大约 10,000 个独特零件和工艺的数量级。”
note_en:
Tesla CEO Elon Musk has warned about battery supply constraints before and the effect it might have on the Tesla Semi, which was first unveiled as a prototype in November 2017. Back in January, Musk said the engineering work on the Tesla Semi had been completed and  deliveries were expected to begin this year. He did add the caveat, at the time, that the availability of battery cells could limit the company’s ability to produce the Semi.
 That warning was apparently warranted. From the shareholders’ letter posted today after the market closed:
  We believe we remain on track to build our first Model Y vehicles in Berlin and Austin in 2021. The pace of the respective production ramps will be influenced by the successful introduction of many new product and manufacturing technologies, ongoing supply-chain-related challenges and regional permitting.
 To better focus on these factories, and due to the limited availability of battery cells and global supply chain challenges, we have shifted the launch of the Semi truck program to 2022. We are also making progress on the industrialization of Cybertruck, which is currently planned for Austin production subsequent to Model Y.
 While not mentioned on the call or in its earnings report, the delay follows the departure of Jerome Guillen, a critical executive at Tesla who was working on the development and eventual production of the Tesla Semi. Guillen’s resignation in June came just three months after he was moved from the president of automotive position, which included oversight of the Tesla Semi, to a role with less responsibility as head of heavy-duty trucking. Guillen had led Tesla’s entire automotive business from September 2018 until March 2021.
 Meanwhile, Tesla’s Cybertruck, which is supposed to go into production in late 2021, looks like it might be pushed into next year as well. Musk didn’t answer questions, but comments from Musk as well as Tesla VP of engineering Lars Moravy during the earnings call suggest that it could shift to 2022.
 Cybertruck is currently in its alpha stages of prototyping, with the basic engineering and architecture of the vehicle completed. While the Model Y takes priority, the company is moving into a beta phase of the Cybertruck later this year, Moravy said.
 “We’ll be looking to ramp that in production in Texas after Model Y is up and going,” he added.
 Musk leaned in on how difficult the Cybertruck would be, perhaps as a way to cushion expectations for its arrival in 2021.
 “Cybertruck ramp will be difficult because it is such a new architecture,” Musk said. “It’s going to be a great product; it might be our best product ever, but it does have a lot of fundamentally new design ideas.”
 He went on to make the point that he has used as other vehicles have gone from prototype to volume production: Manufacturing is hard.
 “At the risk of being repetitive, it’s actually easy to make prototypes or a handful of small-volume production, but anything produced at a high volume, which is really what’s relevant here, is it’s going to move as fast as the slowest of the rough order of magnitude of 10,000 unique parts and processes.”

我们的政府可能正在使用名为 Pegasus 的间谍软件入侵其批评者的手机的消息，引发了一场关于隐私的全新辩论。反对党一有机会就挖苦执政党，而后者在面临如此严重的指控后试图破坏控制。在一片混乱中，飞马计划的一名成员大赦最近制作了一个公共工具包，可以检查您的手机是否感染了飞马。该工具包称为 MVT，它要求用户熟悉命令行。在上一篇文章中，我们写了关于它是如何工作的，并成功地追踪了飞马座的迹象。此外，我们提到了 MVT 如何在 iOS 上比 Android 更有效（您最多只能扫描 APK 和 SMS）。因此，在本指南中，我们专注于将在 iPhone 上检测 Pegasus 的过程分解为分步指南。首先，您需要创建一个加密备份并将其传输到 Mac 或 PC。您也可以在 Linux 上执行此操作，但您必须事先安装 libimobiledevice。手机备份传输后，您需要在系统上下载 Python 3.6（或更新版本）——如果您还没有的话。以下是为 Windows、macOS 和 Linux 安装相同的方法。之后，通过 Amnesty 的手册在您的系统上正确安装 MVT。安装 MVT 将为您提供可以在 Python 命令行中使用的新实用程序（mvt-ios 和 mvt-android）。现在，让我们完成使用 MVT 在 iPhone 备份上检测 Pegasus 的步骤。
首先，您必须解密您的数据备份。为此，您需要输入以下指令格式，同时将占位符文本（标有正斜杠）替换为您的自定义路径。注意：将“/decrypted”替换为要存储解密备份的目录，将“/backup”替换为加密备份所在的目录。现在，我们将对解密的备份运行扫描，使用最新的 IOC（可能是 Pegasus 间谍软件的迹象）引用它，并将结果存储在输出文件夹中。为此，首先，从此处下载最新的 IOC（使用具有最新时间戳的文件夹）。然后，使用您的自定义目录路径输入下面给出的指令格式。注意：将“/output”替换为您要存储扫描结果的目录，将“/backup”替换为存储解密备份的路径，将“/pegasus.stix2”替换为您下载最新 IOC 的路径。扫描完成后，MVT 将在指定的输出文件夹中生成 JSON 文件。如果有一个带有“_detected”后缀的 JSON 文件，那么这意味着你的 iPhone 数据很可能被 Pegasus 感染。然而，国际特赦组织的团队会定期更新 IOC，因为他们对 Pegasus 的运作方式有了更好的了解。因此，您可能希望在 IOC 更新时继续运行扫描，以确保没有误报。
note_en:
The revelation that our government might be using spyware called Pegasus to hack into its critics’ phones has started a  whole new debate on privacy. The opposition is taking a dig at the ruling party every chance it gets, while the latter is trying to damage control after facing such serious allegations.
 Amidst the chaos, one of the members of  The Pegasus Project, Amnesty, recently made a public toolkit that can check if your phone is infected with Pegasus. The toolkit, known as MVT, requires users to know their way around the command line.
    In a previous post, we wrote about  how it works and successfully traces signs of Pegasus. Moreover, we mentioned how MVT is more effective on iOS than Android (the most you can do is scan APKs and SMSes). Hence, in this guide, we’re focusing on breaking down the process to detect Pegasus on iPhone into a step-by-step guide.
 First off, you’ll need to  create an encrypted backup and  transfer it to either a Mac or PC. You can also do this on Linux instead, but you’ll have to  install  libimobiledevice beforehand for that.
 Once the phone backup is transferred, you need to  download Python 3.6 (or newer) on your system — if you don’t have it already. Here’s how you can install the same for  Windows,  macOS, and  Linux.
  After that, go through  Amnesty’s manual to install MVT correctly on your system. Installing MVT will give you new utilities (mvt-ios and mvt-android) that you can use in the Python command line.
 Now, let’s go through the steps for detecting Pegasus on an iPhone backup using MVT.
  First of all, you have to decrypt your data backup. To do that, you’ll need to enter the following instruction format while replacing the placeholder text (marked with a forward slash) with your custom path.
  Note: Replace “/decrypted” with the directory where you want to store the decrypted backup and “/backup” with the directory where your encrypted backup is located.
  Now, we will run a scan on the decrypted backup, referencing it with the latest IOCs (possible signs of Pegasus spyware), and store the result in an output folder.
 To do this, first, download the newest IOCs from  here (use the folder with the latest timestamp). Then, enter the instruction format as given below with your custom directory path.
  Note: Replace “/output” with the directory where you want to store the scan result, “/backup” with the path where your decrypted backup is stored, and “/pegasus.stix2” with the path where you downloaded the latest IOCs.
 After the scan completion, MVT will generate JSON files in the specified output folder. If there is a JSON file with the suffix “_detected,” then that means your iPhone data is most likely Pegasus-infected.
  However, the IOCs are regularly updated by Amnesty’s team as they develop a better understanding of how Pegasus operates. So, you might want to keep running scans as the IOCs are updated to make sure there are no false positives.
